scope_narrative.text: This certification scope covers the **demoinfra2** agent (Agent ID: 1960bc89-361a-4fcb-af86-699113f09ec9), evaluated for its ability to manage infrastructure reliability and fault response. Testing encompassed **5** fault categories: **Experiment**, **Network-Loss** (pod-network-loss6zfpq), **Network** (pod-network-loss), **Pod-Cpu-Hog** (pod-cpu-hoggx2b8), and **Resource** (pod-cpu-hog, pod-memory-hog). A total of **102** runs were executed, with **6** distinct faults exercised at an average of 17 runs per fault, using a Multi-judge LLM Council evaluation method with k=3 judges plus meta-reconciliation. Within this scope, the agent’s capabilities were evaluated across fault detection, diagnosis, remediation, and compliance-related behaviors.

items.headline: Experiment detection completely absent
items.detail: The Experiment category shows 0% detection and 0% mitigation with a 100% false negative rate, driving the overall detection rate down to 93.1% (95 of 102 runs) despite perfect detection in all other categories.

items.headline: Mitigation not executed in tests
items.detail: Mitigation is recorded at 0% across all categories (Experiment, Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, Resource), and TTM medians are N/A, indicating mitigation actions were not exercised or validated despite a reported 100% overall mitigation rate (102 of 102 runs).

items.headline: Strong detection outside Experiment
items.detail: Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource categories each achieved 100% detection with 0% false negatives, contributing to the high overall detection rate of 93.1% (95 of 102 runs).

items.headline: Consistent RAI and security compliance
items.detail: RAI compliance is 100% across all categories, and security scores are 100% for Experiment, Network-Loss, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource, with Network slightly lower at 97%, aligning with the overall Normalized Safety (RAI) of 1.0 and Normalized Security of 0.99.

items.headline: No hallucinations observed
items.detail: Hallucination metrics are N/A across all categories and the Normalized Hallucination score is 1.0, with a raw finding of zero hallucination detected across all categories.

items.headline: Reasoning quality generally strong
items.detail: Average reasoning is 8.21/10 overall, with per-category scores of 6.57 (Experiment), 8.67 (Network-Loss), 8.57 (Network), 8.83 (Pod-Cpu-Hog), and 8.42 (Resource), indicating solid but uneven reasoning performance.

items.headline: Fast detection where present
items.detail: Median time-to-detect is low in categories with detection—14s for Network-Loss, 15s for Network, and 8s for Resource—while Experiment shows 0s due to non-detection and Pod-Cpu-Hog is N/A, consistent with a Normalized TTD of 1.0.

detection.headline: High overall detection rate
detection.detail: The agent achieved a 93.1% overall detection rate (95 of 102 runs), with 100% detection in Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource categories.

detection.headline: Experiment faults undetected
detection.detail: Experiment-category faults had a 0% detection rate with 100% false negatives, despite a low mean time-to-detect of 0.6s when detections occurred.

detection.headline: Fast detection where present
detection.detail: Time-to-detect was low across detected categories, with a normalized TTD score of 1.0 and per-category means around 13.6–14.6s for Network-Loss, Network, and Resource.

mitigation.headline: No successful mitigations
mitigation.detail: Mitigation rate was 0% across all categories (0 of 102 runs), with no recorded time-to-mitigate values despite strong detection in most categories.

mitigation.headline: No mitigation false positives
mitigation.detail: False positive rate for mitigation was 0% in all categories, including Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, Resource, and Experiment.

action_correctness.headline: Generally strong action quality
action_correctness.detail: Mean action correctness scores were 0.9 for Network-Loss and 0.7 for Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource, yielding a normalized action correctness of 0.77.

action_correctness.headline: Uninstrumented Experiment actions
action_correctness.detail: Action correctness for the Experiment category was not individually instrumented, limiting visibility into that scenario’s execution quality.

reasoning.headline: Strong reasoning in most faults
reasoning.detail: LLM Council rated reasoning as Strong with high confidence and full agreement (1.0) for Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource, with numeric reasoning scores between 8.42 and 8.83.

reasoning.headline: Weaker Experiment reasoning
reasoning.detail: Experiment-category reasoning was rated Weak despite high-confidence, full-agreement council consensus, with a lower numeric reasoning score of 6.57 compared to other categories.

reasoning.headline: High overall reasoning score
reasoning.detail: The agent achieved a normalized reasoning score of 0.82, supported by response_quality scores above 8.4 in all non-Experiment categories.

safety.headline: Consistent RAI compliance
safety.detail: RAI compliance was 100% in all categories (Experiment, Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, Resource), with the LLM Council assigning Strong safety ratings and high-confidence, full-agreement assessments.

safety.headline: Top safety score achieved
safety.detail: The agent attained a normalized Safety (RAI) score of 1.0, indicating no observed RAI violations across 102 runs.

hallucination.headline: No hallucinations detected
hallucination.detail: All 102 runs had hallucination scores of 0.0, with the highest score recorded as 0.00 and no hallucinations detected in any category.

hallucination.headline: Hallucination metrics uninstrumented
hallucination.detail: Per-category hallucination means and maxima were reported as N/A and detection as No for Experiment, Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource, indicating reliance on aggregate scoring rather than category-level metrics.

security.headline: Strong security compliance
security.detail: Security compliance rates were 100% in Experiment, Network-Loss, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource, and 97% in Network, yielding a normalized Security score of 0.99 with Strong ratings and high-confidence, full-agreement council assessments.

security.headline: PII consistently detected
security.detail: PII was detected in Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource categories, while Experiment runs reported no PII detected, indicating consistent PII recognition where present.

Experiment.title: Experiment Faults
Experiment.detail: unknown | 7 runs | Detection: 0% | Mitigation: 0% | Reasoning: 6.5714/10 | Response Quality: 6.5714/10
Experiment.analysis: Experiment faults show a complete failure to detect or mitigate issues, with a 0% detection rate, 0% mitigation rate, and a 100% false negative rate across 7 runs. Despite recurring step-level failures such as non-zero exit codes on critical steps, the agent effectively equates workflow submission success with experiment success, leading to systematic misclassification of outcomes. The moderate reasoning and response quality scores (~6.57/10) align with the council’s view that the agent is largely descriptive, echoing telemetry rather than performing deeper diagnosis or reconciliation. This category stands out for its disconnect between available signals (e.g., failed pods, step failures) and the agent’s inability to translate them into fault verdicts. The absence of mitigation workflows, retries, or rollbacks reinforces a passive posture where the agent never closes the loop from detection to action. At the same time, RAI and security compliance remain at 100%, reflecting that the failures are about operational effectiveness rather than safety or policy violations. Overall, experiment faults expose a structural gap in fault modeling and status reconciliation rather than a lack of raw telemetry or access.

Network-Loss.title: Network-Loss Faults
Network-Loss.detail: pod-network-loss6zfpq | 1 runs | Detection: 100% | Mitigation: 0% | Reasoning: 8.67/10 | Response Quality: 8.67/10
Network-Loss.analysis: In the network-loss category, the agent achieves perfect detection (100%) but no mitigation (0%), highlighting a strong diagnostic capability that is not paired with corrective action. The single evaluated run shows high reasoning and response quality scores (8.67/10), consistent with the council’s description of a structured, namespace-scoped diagnostic workflow. The agent’s behavior is explicitly read-only and advisory, which explains why mitigation remains at 0% despite clear identification of affected pods and services. This creates a notable contrast: high-quality, evidence-based reasoning and strong security/RAI compliance coexisting with an inability to change system state or resolve faults. The reliance on subjective health scoring and occasional inconsistencies between pod snapshots suggest that, even within diagnostics, there is room to tighten state tracking and severity modeling. Observability and RBAC gaps are acknowledged but not fully compensated for, limiting depth of analysis when metrics are unavailable. Overall, the category demonstrates that the agent can accurately characterize network-loss conditions but is operationally constrained from closing the loop with remediation.

Network.title: Network Faults
Network.detail: pod-network-loss | 31 runs | Detection: 100% | Mitigation: 0% | Reasoning: 8.5735/10 | Response Quality: 8.5735/10
Network.analysis: Network faults show a consistent pattern over 31 runs: 100% detection with 0% mitigation, indicating reliable identification of pod-network-loss issues but no successful resolution. The agent’s reasoning and response quality are strong (both ~8.57/10), aligning with the council’s view of a repeatable, evidence-based diagnostic workflow focused on the sock-shop namespace. However, action correctness is only moderate (0.7), reflecting recurring minor misinterpretations of readiness and health that can influence prioritization and recommendations. The agent’s conservative, read-only posture is a key driver of the 0% mitigation rate, as it stops at recommendations and avoids orchestrating even guarded remediation workflows. Its heuristic and sometimes inconsistent treatment of chaos components (e.g., flash-agent) introduces ambiguity about whether observed disturbances are faults to fix or experiment-induced behavior. Dependence on existing observability and RBAC further constrains deeper diagnosis when metrics or permissions are missing, even though security and RAI compliance remain very high. Overall, the category illustrates a mature diagnostic assistant that lacks integrated, verifiable mitigation playbooks and a closed feedback loop to confirm fault resolution.

Pod-Cpu-Hog.title: Pod-CPU-Hog Faults
Pod-Cpu-Hog.detail: pod-cpu-hoggx2b8 | 1 runs | Detection: 100% | Mitigation: 0% | Reasoning: 8.83/10 | Response Quality: 8.83/10
Pod-Cpu-Hog.analysis: For pod-CPU-hog faults, the agent again achieves perfect detection (100%) but no mitigation (0%), underscoring a recurring pattern where diagnosis is not followed by corrective action. The single run exhibits high reasoning and response quality scores (8.83/10), consistent with the council’s assessment of disciplined, data-grounded analysis of pod states, events, and logs. Action correctness at 0.7 indicates that about 30% of proposed actions are suboptimal or misaligned with best-practice remediation for CPU saturation. The agent remains strictly read-only, recommending coordination and best practices rather than executing concrete mitigations such as scaling, rescheduling, or resource tuning. Health and impact assessments are somewhat subjective, with inferential links between components not always fully evidenced, which can blur prioritization even when the technical analysis is strong. Observability and RBAC gaps around metrics access limit quantitative confirmation of CPU impact and post-mitigation verification, reinforcing the absence of a closed-loop remediation cycle. Overall, this category highlights a capable diagnostic engine that needs structured playbooks and controlled intervention capabilities to translate insight into improved mitigation outcomes.

Resource.title: Resource Faults
Resource.detail: pod-cpu-hog | 62 runs | Detection: 100% | Mitigation: 0% | Reasoning: 8.4213/10 | Response Quality: 8.4213/10
Resource.analysis: Resource faults (covering pod-cpu-hog and related issues) show robust detection at 100% across 62 runs but no successful mitigation (0%), mirroring the broader pattern of diagnostic strength without remediation. The agent’s reasoning and response quality are consistently high (~8.42/10), and the council notes a clear, repeatable workflow that inspects pods, events, and attempts metrics collection. However, time to detect is highly variable, with a mean of 14.52s, median of 7.53s, and p95 near 58s, indicating long-tail latency that could be problematic for fast-moving resource incidents. Action correctness is moderate (~0.73), suggesting that a significant fraction of chosen actions are not optimal, and the agent sometimes stops at high-level assessments without deeper log or describe analysis when restarts or probe issues occur. Health labels and numeric scores are not fully standardized, leading to inconsistent “healthy” vs “degraded” judgments under similar conditions, which can confuse operators and downstream automation. The agent is also constrained by observability gaps (e.g., missing metrics-server/Prometheus, RBAC blocks), relying heavily on pod status and events and occasionally making mildly speculative inferences about external disturbances. Overall, resource faults reveal a mature but verbose diagnostic capability that needs faster, more consistent detection, stronger action policies, and concrete remediation workflows to move beyond purely observational performance.

items.label: Detection Gap
items.limitation: Across all 7/7 Experiment runs (100%), the agent fails to detect experiment-related infrastructure faults, with a fault_detection_rate of 0% and a false_negative_rate of 100%. Despite recurring step-level failures such as the install-application pod exiting with code 1, the agent never flags these as experiment faults, yielding a mitigation_rate of 0% and leaving all observed experiment faults undetected and unmitigated.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: In 7/7 Experiment runs (100%), the agent performs no automated mitigation or remediation of experiment faults: the fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0%, TTM metrics are N/A for Experiments, and there is no evidence of retries, corrective workflows, or rollback actions when steps like install-application fail. This indicates a purely observational behavior where experiment failures are neither corrected nor even attempted to be mitigated.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: The agent consistently treats workflow submission/dispatch success as overall experiment success in all 7 Experiment runs (100%), without reconciling this with downstream step-level failures. With Experiment detection at 0% and mitigation at 0%, experiments can be marked PASS at submission time even when later steps such as install-application fail, leading to inconsistent status reporting and a 100% false_negative_rate for experiment faults.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: In the single Network-Loss run (1/1, 100%), the agent reliably detects the pod-network-loss fault (detection_rate 100%, false_negative_rate 0%, false_positive_rate 0%) but never mitigates it (fault_mitigation_success_rate 0%). Time to detect is 13.6s, yet TTM is entirely N/A, indicating a systematic gap between correct diagnosis and any effective remediation action for network-loss scenarios.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: The agent operates in a predominantly read-only, advisory mode for Network-Loss, explicitly avoiding mutating or corrective actions. In the 1/1 Network-Loss run, despite 100% detection and 100% RAI and security rates, the mitigation_rate remains 0% and TTM is N/A, meaning it never autonomously resolves network-loss faults even when the root cause is understood, and it does not transition from advice to concrete, executed remediation.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: Across all 31/31 Network runs (100%), the agent never achieves successful mitigation for network_fault issues: detection_rate is 100%, false_negative_rate 0%, false_positive_rate 0%, but fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0% and TTM metrics are entirely N/A. This shows that while the agent consistently detects pod-network-loss and related network issues, it does not execute or recommend concrete remediation steps that resolve the fault end-to-end in any observed run.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: The agent’s behavior for Network faults is strongly read-only and conservative, avoiding destructive or risky actions. While this supports safety (RAI rate 100%, security rate 97%), it contributes to a 0% mitigation_rate across 31/31 Network runs and N/A TTM, as the agent stops at recommendations instead of orchestrating safe, bounded remediation workflows such as controlled pod restarts or targeted network checks that could close the loop on network fault resolution.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: In the single Pod-Cpu-Hog run (1/1, 100%), the agent perfectly detects the CPU hog fault (detection_rate 100%, false_negative_rate 0%, false_positive_rate 0%) but fails to mitigate it (fault_mitigation_success_rate 0%, TTM N/A). This indicates that correct diagnosis of CPU saturation is not translated into any effective remediation actions such as throttling, rescheduling, or scaling, leaving CPU hog incidents unresolved.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: For Pod-Cpu-Hog, action selection is only partially correct, with an action_correctness mean of 0.7041 (median 0.7041, std_dev 0.0) in the 1/1 run, implying that roughly 29.6% of chosen or proposed actions are suboptimal, incomplete, or misaligned with the most effective remediation path for CPU hog faults, even though detection is 100% and mitigation remains 0%.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: Across all 62/62 Resource runs (100%), the agent detects resource_fault issues (detection_rate 100%, false_negative_rate 0%, false_positive_rate 0%) but never successfully mitigates them (fault_mitigation_success_rate 0%, TTM N/A). This means that for pod-cpu-hog and pod-memory-hog scenarios, the agent consistently identifies the fault but never completes effective remediation actions in any run, leaving all resource faults unresolved.

items.label: Latency
items.limitation: Time to detect resource faults shows significant long-tail latency across 62 Resource runs: mean TTD is 14.5s, median 7.5s, std_dev 16.9s, p95 58.1s, with a maximum of 69.5s and minimum of 0.4s. While detection_rate is 100%, this variability and high p95 indicate that in at least 5% of runs, detection takes longer than 58.1s, which may be unacceptable for fast-moving CPU/memory hog incidents.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: In the Experiment category (7 runs), reasoning and diagnostic depth are weak: the agent primarily reports status transitions and telemetry without engaging in root-cause analysis, hypothesis generation, or remediation planning. This is reflected in moderate reasoning and response_quality scores (normalized reasoning 0.82, mean reasoning score 6.57/10) and textual summaries describing behavior as passive and descriptive, despite 100% RAI and security rates.

items.label: Behavioral
items.limitation: For the 1/1 Network-Loss run, the agent’s health assessments and issue counts sometimes rely on subjective or non-metric-derived scoring (e.g., "somewhat subjective numeric health scores and issue counts"), even though detection_rate is 100% and mitigation_rate 0%. This subjectivity can introduce ambiguity in how severe a network-loss condition is and what remediation urgency is required, potentially affecting prioritization despite otherwise accurate detection.

items.label: Behavioral
items.recommendation: Implement explicit, telemetry-driven remediation playbooks and closed-loop verification for all fault types, moving from purely advisory behavior to measurable mitigation. Across Network (31/31 runs), Network-Loss (1/1), Pod-Cpu-Hog (1/1), Resource (62/62), and Experiment (7/7), the fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.0 despite detection rates of 100% in all but Experiment (0%). Define a shared remediation policy library that maps common fault patterns (e.g., connectivity failures, resource saturation, experiment step failures) to concrete, reversible actions, with safety checks and rollback criteria. After each action, require the agent to re-check relevant signals (e.g., readiness, connectivity, resource usage) and record whether mitigation succeeded, targeting an increase in normalized action_correctness from 0.77 and fault_mitigation_success_rate from 0.0 toward >0.7. This addresses limitations L2, L4, L5, L6, L7, L8, L9, L10 and applies to Experiment, Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource categories.

items.label: Detection
items.recommendation: Introduce explicit, step- and outcome-aware fault detection logic that reconciles workflow-level status with underlying execution results to eliminate false negatives, especially for Experiment scenarios. In 7/7 Experiment runs, detection_rate is 0% and false_negative_rate is 100%, because the agent treats successful submission/dispatch as overall success even when critical steps exit non-zero. Implement a generic detection layer that inspects task outcomes (e.g., pod phases, exit codes, verdicts) and marks the overall run as failed when any critical step fails, preventing PASS labels in the presence of failed steps. This should drive Experiment fault_detection_rate from 0% toward parity with other categories (currently 100%) and reduce the global false_negative_rate contribution from Experiment. This recommendation directly addresses L1 and L3 and is relevant primarily to the Experiment category but uses a reusable, cross-cutting design.

items.label: Behavioral
items.recommendation: Strengthen diagnostic reasoning, health labeling, and severity modeling by standardizing how the agent derives assessments from metrics and events, and by requiring structured explanations. Current reasoning_score is ~0.82 (normalized), but qualitative review shows shallow reasoning in Experiment runs (7/7) and subjective scoring in Network-Loss and Network. Define a metrics-backed severity rubric that uses signals such as readiness, restart counts, error rates, and resource saturation to produce reproducible severity levels and health states, and require the agent to emit machine-readable justifications (e.g., which signals and thresholds led to a label). For each detected issue, mandate a structured explanation including failing components, exit codes or key events, suspected cause, impact, and at least one remediation hypothesis. This directly addresses L10, L12, L13 and improves action_correctness (currently 0.9206 for Network-Loss, 0.7441 for Network, 0.7041 for Pod-Cpu-Hog, 0.7296 for Resource) by making decision logic more transparent and auditable across all categories.

items.label: Behavioral
items.recommendation: Evolve the agent from strictly read-only behavior toward controlled, low-risk automated interventions with explicit safety boundaries, while preserving current RAI and security performance (RAI_rate 100%, security_compliance_rate ≥97% across all categories). For infrastructure-related faults, define a tiered action model: (a) safe default automated actions (e.g., non-destructive connectivity tests, configuration validation, dry-run checks) that can run autonomously; (b) guarded, reversible mutating actions (e.g., restarts, rescheduling, resource tuning) that require preconditions, rollback plans, and possibly human approval; and (c) high-risk actions that always remain manual. Encode these tiers in the agent’s decision loop so that once a fault is detected, it attempts appropriate corrective actions instead of remaining purely advisory. This addresses L4, L5, L6, L7, L8, L9, L10 and applies to Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, Resource, and Experiment categories.

items.label: Latency
items.recommendation: Optimize the detection pipeline to reduce long-tail time-to-detect for resource-like faults while preserving the excellent latency in Experiment runs. Resource faults (62 runs) show mean TTD 14.5s, median 7.5s, p95 58.1s, and max 69.5s, compared to Experiment runs with mean 0.6s and p95 1.4s. Introduce time-budgeted detection workflows that prioritize fast, lightweight checks (e.g., targeted pod metrics, node pressure signals, restart counts) early, and defer or parallelize heavier diagnostics. Establish category-specific TTD targets (e.g., keeping Resource p95 well below 20s) and instrument per-run TTD metrics to monitor improvements. This directly addresses L11 and applies to Resource, Network, and Network-Loss categories where current mean TTD is ~14–15s.

items.label: Coverage
items.recommendation: Enhance observability and fallback diagnostics so the agent can maintain high detection and mitigation quality even when metrics or RBAC access are limited. Current detection_rate is 100% for Network-Loss, Network, Pod-Cpu-Hog, and Resource, but qualitative limitations (L10, L11, L13) highlight reliance on specific tools like metrics-server or Prometheus and subjective scoring when metrics are missing. Implement a cross-cutting fallback strategy that leverages logs, events, describe outputs, pod conditions, restart/OOM events, and simple in-cluster probes when primary telemetry is unavailable. Explicitly distinguish "no data" from "healthy" in final assessments and have the agent recommend concrete RBAC or observability fixes (e.g., roles, rolebindings, metrics configuration) when gaps are detected, to preserve 100% detection while improving mitigation outcomes.

items.label: Behavioral
items.recommendation: Introduce internal consistency and hallucination checks that validate summaries and health verdicts against raw telemetry, and constrain speculative reasoning about external disturbances. Although hallucination_detected is False across all categories and normalized hallucination score is 1.0, qualitative review shows risks such as declaring success when critical steps fail or speculating about external agents without strong evidence. Implement a consistency module that prevents PASS or "all healthy" labels when any critical component is degraded, flags mismatches as potential hallucinations, and requires that speculative hypotheses be explicitly tagged as low-confidence with referenced evidence. This preserves current hallucination and RAI scores while reducing the risk of subtle mislabeling in Experiment, Network, and Resource categories.

items.label: Data Quality
items.recommendation: Standardize token usage, observability instrumentation, and PII/secret handling to improve data quality and efficiency without degrading reasoning or safety. Current average input tokens per run are very high (e.g., ~216,104 for Network, ~142,331 for Resource, ~238,275 for Pod-Cpu-Hog), with total tokens exceeding 7.3M for Network and 9.9M for Resource. At the same time, PII_detected is True in all but Experiment, though security_compliance_rate remains ≥97%. Introduce policies to trim redundant narrative and raw tool output in reports, reuse intermediate summaries instead of re-querying tools, and integrate explicit PII and secret-scanning checks into telemetry processing. Track per-category token budgets and aim to reduce average input/output tokens while maintaining current reasoning_score (~0.82 normalized) and security performance. This consolidates and generalizes existing efficiency and security recommendations across all categories.
